metadata.narrative_arc: A developer opens a legacy Python repo for the first time, asks the agent to map the module structure and flag code smells, and closes with a rough triage list but unresolved anxiety about the apparent absence of any test coverage.

events.description: User shares the repo root path and says: 'I just inherited this Python project. No docs, no handover. Can you map out the module structure and tell me what we're dealing with?'

Agent recursively lists directories and files, identifies six top-level packages (billing, reporting, utils, integrations, scripts, config) and produces a brief summary of each package's apparent responsibility based on file names and import statements.

Agent scans all .py files for common code smells: finds bare except clauses in billing/processor.py, a 430-line God function in reporting/builder.py, multiple hard-coded credentials in config/settings.py, and no __init__.py in the integrations package. Agent notes the principle 'always read tests before touching source' and goes looking for a tests/ directory — it does not exist. There is no test suite at all.

User reacts with surprise: 'Wait, there are literally zero tests? Not even a placeholder? And someone hard-coded credentials in settings.py?' User asks whether 'small focused commits over large refactors' still makes sense if the foundation itself is this fragile.

Agent compiles a prioritised triage list: (1) rotate hard-coded credentials immediately, (2) add a minimal pytest scaffold before any source edits, (3) isolate and document the God function in reporting/builder.py before splitting it, (4) add __init__.py to integrations. Agent flags that deeper refactoring decisions should wait until at least smoke tests exist.

key_moments.what_happened: When the agent finished mapping the six packages and summarising each one's role, the codebase shifted from a black box to something with recognisable boundaries. The structure was messy but now at least visible.
key_moments.why_it_matters: Clarity is a core value here; moving from total uncertainty to a rough map — even of a troubled codebase — is a genuine step forward and makes subsequent decisions possible.
principles_confirmed: trust existing naming conventions until proven wrong
key_moments.what_changed: The repo went from opaque inheritance to a mapped, nameable set of concerns.

key_moments.what_happened: Discovering that there is no test directory at all — while simultaneously holding the principle 'always read tests before touching source' — created a direct conflict: the principle assumes tests exist to read.
key_moments.why_it_matters: The guiding principle 'always read tests before touching source' becomes practically meaningless when there is nothing to read; the session ended without resolving whether to write tests first or accept touching untested source as a necessary bootstrapping step.
principles_questioned: always read tests before touching source
small focused commits over large refactors
key_moments.what_changed: The working assumption that some safety net exists was removed; every subsequent action in this repo carries more risk than anticipated.

expected_session_outcome.key_insight: The repo is navigable but has no test coverage whatsoever, which undermines the standard 'read tests first' principle and means the triage list must begin with scaffolding tests rather than any source change.
